post_text: The most underrated skill in business is not communication or leadership or time management. It is learning how to be wrong GRACEFULLY. Anyone can say "I embrace failure" in a calm moment alone but the real test is what you do in the exact second you realise you were wrong and someone else is watching. Because in that moment every instinct in you wants to defend the position or shift the blame slightly or explain why you were not entirely wrong and those instincts are fast and they are loud and most people follow them without even noticing... I have been in rooms where I was confident about a direction and the data came in and it said otherwise and what I did in that moment mattered more to my team than the original decision ever did! Because people do not follow leaders who are always right. They follow leaders who are honest when they are not.

I put a PE backed company in rehab. Then the market collapsed. That was one of the hardest lessons of my career. I was brought in because the business was already in serious trouble. Years of weak decisions had left deep damage: poor operational discipline, weak financial structure, little strategic flexibility, almost no financial buffer. My job was to help stabilize and turn it around. And for a while, it worked. Lead times improved. Productivity improved. Quality improved. The team started believing again. It felt like the patient was finally breathing without support. Then reality changed faster than any turnaround plan can account for.

Last August, a founder I spoke with this week deliberately cut his monthly revenue from $110,000 to $10,000. His co-founder thought he'd lost his mind. He'd built a data and AI services business fast — first client a $30B hedge fund, next a PE firm, next a major investment bank. Classic services ramp. But somewhere in there he noticed something. Even his best clients — the ones who praised them publicly, sent referrals, called them their best friends — were engaging them project by project. Every new statement of work was essentially starting over. The relationships were strong. The business model was fragile. So he called each client and said: sign a 12-month contract to move onto our hosted platform, or we need to part ways. Almost no one converted. He walked away from nearly all the revenue anyway.

My business went from completely stagnant 10k months to 50k months for clients and 100k months quite literally overnight once I implemented this one pricing strategy, and you can use the exact same thing in your business. Now it's known as a value ladder, and here's how it works. At the top you have your main service, your core service, the service that you inevitably want to sell. This should be priced properly, ideally in the $5,000 to $10,000 a month range. If you can't price at that point, then you've either got a value problem or a confidence problem. This is where you want to get to. Before that, you've got more steps on the ladder. The whole idea is that people who don't know you don't typically like to be sold and charged five figures for a service.

Most people never realize they're terrible listeners until it costs them something important. A promotion they deserved. A relationship that mattered. A team that stopped being honest with them. I've watched leaders lose talented people not because of strategy or compensation, but because their team never felt heard. And I've been that leader. My wife will tell you I've perfected the art of nodding while mentally drafting emails. She's called me out more than once for asking a question she literally just answered. 'Were you even listening?' The honest answer? No. I was performing listening while planning something else. Real listening happens in layers. Most of us get stuck at the surface.

If you're a consultant, coach, or advisor, this one is for you. At some point in most consulting careers, a pattern emerges: You walk into a business. You diagnose the problem. You fix it. They pay you for your time. You leave. They keep all the value you created. For a while, that feels fine. Then it starts to feel like something's off. What if instead of billing for your expertise, you took equity? What if instead of advising on acquisitions, you did them? The shift from fees to equity isn't simple. It requires a different mindset, a different relationship with risk, and a very different set of conversations with potential partners. But for the right person, it changes everything. I've seen consultants make this shift inside 12 months and complete their first acquisition.

From 40 consultants to 6. That's not a future scenario. It's already happening. PwC recently completed a project that once needed 40 people with just six. Same outcome. Less time. Far fewer people. That's the current reality. This is how AI is changing work. Quietly. Quickly. It doesn't always remove entire jobs overnight. It shrinks the amount of work behind each job. And when that happens, companies don't need the same team size anymore. This is where many professionals get caught off guard. You still have your role. But the value of what you do is being compressed. Tasks like research, analysis, slide preparation, and reporting are now faster. Cheaper. Sometimes automated. So the real question is no longer: Will AI take my job? It's this: If AI does 60% of my work, why would the company need me full time?

Most LinkedIn personal-brand advice makes you worse at business. I'm done pretending it isn't. If you open LinkedIn today, you'll get the same five voices selling the same blueprints. 5 fundamentals for $100k months. 10 rules to viral content. The hook-story-lesson formula. Post 5 times a week for 12 months. Step 1, step 2, step 3. All it does is dress you up like everyone else.

"Consulting is dead. Here are 3 prompts that replace what McKinsey charges $500k for." I keep seeing these types of posts on LinkedIn. And it gets more ridiculous every time. Consulting is not dying. In fact it has survived everything thrown at it. In the 1990s, SAP and Oracle were supposed to make consultants redundant. ERP systems would codify how businesses ran and eliminate expensive advisors. Who needs consultants when the software thinks for you? What actually happened: ERP created one of the largest consulting booms in history. The software was complex, the implementations were brutal, and the organizations needed help.

Hot take: most B2B SaaS companies don't have a sales problem. They have a positioning problem that is showing up in the pipeline as a sales problem. And they're solving it by hiring more salespeople. Pipeline is thin. Reps are working hard. Close rates are below benchmark. The founder is in every major deal. The instinctive response: better salespeople. More training. A new VP Sales. A new process. These responses are correct if the problem is execution. They are expensive and demoralising if the problem is that the ICP doesn't believe your positioning enough to move forward.

Next time you're hiring, do this. Go to their LinkedIn profile, click comments. See what they say when they think it doesn't matter, because it does matter. I've been thinking about this after watching a fellow female founder get a genuinely disgusting comment on a completely normal photo yesterday. A person with an open to work banner on his profile decided a professional platform was the right place to be publicly demeaning to a woman trying to build something. I wonder how many jobs they're applying for where the employer wouldn't even see this behaviour until they're employed. But that comment lives on his profile, permanently, and if they ever applied to work at striveX, I'd find it before they got through the first stage.

The first question I ask every new client: What's on fire? Nothing, Darren! All good! We're absolutely grand! ...tumbleweed... Well... There is this one thing. There always is. Usually it's one of three things: Something slightly scary has already happened and nobody knows how to handle it. Another founder told them to sort out your people stuff before it becomes a mess. Or something is coming around the corner fast — a round closing, a senior hire, a restructure — and they don't want to get it wrong. So we start there. And that one thing? Usually bigger than they'd realised. What looks like a small people issue is usually a ticking clock - the kind that gets very loud, very fast if nobody's paying attention to it.

If you have leads and you can't close them, but your existing customers love what you're doing, that's not a sales problem. It's a positioning problem. I say this because most teams, when they're struggling to close, immediately go looking for better reps, more outbound volume, a new sequence. They optimize the top of the funnel or they optimize the closer. But they never ask the most important question: do the people we're talking to actually understand what we do and why it matters to them? The first thing I look at when I step into a new revenue org isn't the pipeline. It's what customers are doing with the product. Do they stay? Do they expand? Do they refer others? Because that tells me whether the value is real.

THANK YOU FOR A 1,000,000 NOs! 5.5 years at McKinsey & Company taught me my ideas were valuable. 22 months building Dalil AI taught me that wasn't enough. At McKinsey, I was advising billion-dollar companies on multi-million deals. Every suggestion felt valuable. Every decision mattered. I was in rooms with C-level executives. They listened, I spoke, things happened. That's not how entrepreneurship works. In the last 22 months, I've been told NO more times than I can count. - NO from customers who didn't want to pay $80/month. - NO from investors who passed on a $5k check. - NO from partners negotiating over a 5% commission difference. - NO from talents who had better options. Not millions on the line. 80 dollars a month. The first few hit differently than you'd expect. You've built something real. You believe in it. Why don't they? Then I realized. At McKinsey, I was selling my analysis. As a founder, I'm selling my belief.

Full-time ServiceNow architect at $175K/year: After taxes: ~$125K. One employer, one income stream. Limited upside. Annual raises of 3-5%. Benefits included. Independent ServiceNow consultant at $165/hr: 1,600 billable hours/year = $264K gross. After taxes and expenses: ~$185K. Multiple clients, diversified income. Unlimited upside. Rate increases of 10-20% per engagement. No benefits (but you can buy better ones). The math is not close. But independence is not for everyone. You need pipeline. You need a brand. You need to be comfortable with gaps between contracts. The biggest mistake I see is people going independent to escape their situation, not run toward something.

Boutique consulting firms do not fail to scale because of a lack of market demand. They fail because the founder becomes the ultimate bottleneck. When advising boutique consultancies, I frequently observe partners stuck in the "expert trap". They equate quality with personal execution, keeping authority centralised long after the firm has outgrown its initial startup phase. If you do not delegate, you cap your firm's value. But if you delegate without the right infrastructure, you trigger a cycle of poor delivery, stressed staff, and senior rework. Research by Kaiser and Ringlstetter (2010) on professional service firm configuration demonstrates that as firms grow, they must transition from informal, founder-led oversight to structured management systems. Without this architectural shift, the firm hits a severe coordination crisis that stalls growth. True delegation requires specific, deliberate operational infrastructure to support junior delivery without sacrificing client satisfaction. First, productise your intellectual property. If your firm's unique value exists only in the minds of the partners, junior consultants cannot replicate it. Documented frameworks and delivery playbooks serve as your quality baseline. Second, pivot middle management from execution to enabling. Senior delivery staff must shift their focus from doing the work to quality assurance and mentoring. This means training them how to review work constructively, rather than simply taking over and rewriting deliverables when a junior struggles. Third, map your functional competencies. You need a clear matrix outlining exactly what a consultant can do independently at each grade level. This eliminates guesswork about who is ready for specific project responsibilities. Finally, align your rewards with leverage. If your senior delivery staff are only measured on personal fee utilisation, they have no commercial incentive to mentor juniors. KPIs must explicitly reward the development of others. Effective delegation is not an abdication of responsibility: it is a structured architecture that transfers capability and builds genuine enterprise value.

I'm increasingly tired of being told that "AI will replace most white-collar jobs" by people who haven't spent a single day actually doing those jobs. Many of the same people told me that all Uber cars would be self-driving by 2024. And their parents' generation were told offices would become paperless yet fax machines are still being made in 2026. The loudest voices in the AI hype cycle understand the technology just enough to be dangerous, and the domain not at all. They see a polished demo of an AI "agent" booking a meeting, drafting an email, or pulling a few numbers from a spreadsheet and immediately extrapolate to "this will replace accountants, lawyers, consultants, and analysts within five years." Anyone who has actually closed a messy multi-entity month-end, navigated conflicting regulations, negotiated stakeholder tradeoffs, or made judgment calls in ambiguous data knows how absurd that leap is. Real work is not a sequence of clean API calls; it is tacit knowledge, negotiated context, incomplete information, politics, and responsibility when things go wrong. The people promising AI will replace entire professions rarely demonstrate any understanding of that substrate. They confuse "can draft a plausible-looking document" with "can be accountable for a decision." I'm not anti-AI. I use these systems heavily and I think they will reshape knowledge work, primarily by changing the cost structure of specific tasks rather than deleting whole careers. But I am very much anti hand-wavy, industrial-scale Dunning-Kruger: investors and thought leaders confidently predicting the end of professional expertise while showing, sentence after sentence, that they've never done the work they're dismissing. AI will absolutely replace a certain kind of white-collar activity. The kind built on shallow pattern-matching, cargo-cult PowerPoints, and insights reverse-engineered from the conclusion slide. For the rest of us, the bar will rise. Judgment, domain depth, and the ability to integrate messy reality will matter more, not less. I'm worried that credulous leadership will listen to people who don't understand either AI or actual work, and will burn time, money, and trust chasing a fantasy instead of building the future at human scale.

A client came to me earning $200K as an Architect at a global consulting firm in Sydney. 3 months later, he accepted a Principal role at one of Australia's most recognised tech companies. $460K+ annual package. More than double. So no, the market didn't "suddenly get better." He stopped showing up like a safe candidate. And started showing up like the obvious hire. Here's what was killing his pay: He was describing work. Not selling outcomes. His resume read like a Jira ticket history. His interview answers sounded like fortnightly steering committee updates. So hiring managers did what they always do: They grouped him with everyone else. "Strong candidate." "Solid background." "Let's keep looking." So we rebuilt everything. 1) Positioning — We extracted his unfair advantage and built a sharp value proposition. Not another line about leading cross-functional teams. 2) Proof — We rewrote his stories into tight narratives that made hiring managers think: This person has solved our exact problem before. At scale. 3) Access + Interview stance — He stopped answering like an applicant reciting KPIs. He started leading like a consultant. Diagnose. Clarify. Recommend. Make the decision feel safe. We also engineered insider access before interviews even happened. And then came the offer. Over $430K AUD package. He was too excited to say YES. But we took a pause and negotiated the offer: +$10K on base +$50K USD on equity. The base increase alone more than covered his coaching investment. You don't get paid more because you're qualified. You get paid more when the market believes you're the lowest-risk path to the outcome. That's what separates $200K from $460K. If you're earning $180K-$220K with 20 years behind you, and you've been telling yourself next year I'll sort it out — you've probably been saying that for 3 years. DM LEAP if you want in.

A client paid me $1,800 over 18 months. They closed $180,000 in new business because of my work. I didn't see the problem at the time. I didn't charge this little because the value wasn't there — clearly it was. But because I never stopped to do the math on what I was actually worth to the people paying me. When I finally did, I raised my price to $1,799/month. Same service. Same clients. Nearly 18x the revenue. Here's what nobody tells you about raising your prices: the right clients don't leave. They take you more seriously. They do the work. They get better results. The ones who push back? They were never going to get the results anyway. I ran that agency until I sold it in 2022. The pricing shift was one of the things that made it sellable. Now I coach agency owners. And my goal is simple: every client I work with should see at least $300,000 in new value in their first year. If the math doesn't work for them, I'm not the right fit. I was stuck at $100/month because I had no way to connect my price to the value I delivered. Once I had that framework, everything changed. Comment PRICING and I'll send you a free calculator that shows you what you should actually be charging — based on your real numbers.

I've turned down AI-in-HR projects worth real money. Not because the work wasn't interesting. Because I could see, in the first conversation, that the project would fail. Three buyer profiles I now decline: 1. Top-down pressure, no CHRO ownership: The CEO read a McKinsey report on the flight back. The board wants AI in HR in the next deck. The CHRO is being told to deliver something, anything. In that room, AI isn't a strategy. It's performance. The moment something breaks, I'm the one blamed for a project nobody actually wanted. 2. No internal capability to execute: We can build the agent. Configure the platform. Run the pilot. But if nobody on the client side can own the workflow after handover, it dies six weeks later. AI in HR isn't plug-and-play. It needs an internal owner. If that person doesn't exist, no consultant can fill the gap permanently. 3. The reactive stance: The most dangerous client is the one who hires you to prove AI won't work. Arms crossed. Questions that are actually objections. Failed pilots are already blamed for the tool. These engagements aren't designed to succeed. They're designed to confirm a belief. The company experience sits with me. Org was ready on paper. Budget there. Leadership open. But I could see the change fatigue from three back-to-back platform rollouts. Adding AI on top would have broken what we'd just built. I said no. The hardest skill in consulting isn't selling the engagement. It's reading whether it can succeed before you take the money. If you're a CHRO evaluating an AI partner, the better question isn't what can you build? It's what would make you walk away from this project? The honest answer tells you everything.

Confidence is a professional requirement in consulting, but it is also a strategic liability. Based on my work with boutique consultancy boards, on growth and M&A, I frequently see a specific risk: the transition from the confident practitioner to the unconsciously incompetent leader. We are paid for answers. Our early careers reward the ability to synthesise information quickly and speak with authority. This is a vital skill for client delivery, but it inadvertently cultivates a version of the Dunning-Kruger effect. Leaders in boutique firms are particularly vulnerable because they must wear multiple hats, acting as head of HR, lead negotiator, and strategy director, often without deep functional expertise in each domain. To protect the equity value of your firm, you must identify when conviction is actually bias. Intellectual rigour requires you to doubt an internal strategy when it meets any of these five criteria: Empirical Conflict — the strategy is contradicted by verifiable benchmarks or market data. Structural Inconsistency — there are logical gaps in the if-then chain of your business case. Ideological Bias — the position is driven by how we have always done it rather than current market reality. Expert Dissent — the consensus among external domain experts differs from your internal view. Domain Distance — you are making a high-stakes decision far outside your primary area of professional training. In the pursuit of strategic truth, we must recognise that gut feel is often a mask for hidden prejudice.

I've seen some things. A vendor show up to a meeting - the wrong meeting - in the wrong building - for the wrong company - and somehow still try to pitch us. I've seen a six inch water line burst and flood a warehouse. I've seen a pallet of product fall off a dock and land perfectly upright like it was making a statement. I've seen a CEO show up to a food grade facility with a drink in hand that I had to confiscate (that went well). I've seen things that no SOP could have prepared anyone for. Things that would make a Harvard case study question its own methodology. Things that happened on a Tuesday for no reason at all. And yet - after all of it - the floods, the failures, the audacious decisions, the moments that had no business working but somehow did - here's what I've actually learned: Most things that feel like a crisis aren't. Most situations that look unique - aren't. Most problems that seem impossible have already been solved somewhere and by someone with fewer resources and a tighter deadline. The details change. The patterns don't. Experience doesn't make you unshakeable. It just makes the shaking stop a lot faster. I've seen some things. And the most useful thing all of it gave me wasn't a solution for every situation. It was the calm to know I'd figure it out anyway.

A business consultant came to me 10 weeks ago spending $8,000 per month on ads with a 0.9x ROAS. The ads were technically fine. The landing page was the problem. It was written for people who already understood the value of consulting and were actively looking to hire. Cold traffic does not arrive in that state. We rewrote the page to meet cold traffic where it actually is: skeptical, busy, and not yet convinced they need what you are selling. Ten weeks later: $34,000 in new consulting contracts from ads in a single month. The budget did not change. The targeting did not change. The page and the offer framing changed. If your high-ticket ads are spending without converting, start with the page.

Most boutique consultancies leave 1–2 turns of EBITDA on the table. Not because the numbers are wrong. Because they go to market on last year's P&L instead of deliberately engineering the drivers that compress risk and expand multiples over the 18 months before exit. What buyers actually pay for is not complicated: higher recurring revenue, lower client concentration, sustained growth with credible margins, real vertical depth in complex sectors, proprietary IP and accelerators, a clear plan for succession. That is what reduces risk in diligence. That is what expands multiples. The 2026 twist is that the AI story has to be architectural, not cosmetic. We use Copilot is table stakes. What commands a premium is harder to replicate: proprietary data assets, methodology-plus-model combinations that improve delivery over time, and revenue per consultant moving up as the model scales. That is the difference between AI-enabled and AI-washed diligence. Two levers are still underrated. First, productise one service line. Once part of the business starts to look like repeatable tech-enabled delivery rather than pure professional services, buyers start to value that revenue differently. Second, build the narrative as a platform, not a practice. Sponsors are willing to pay for boutiques that can absorb bolt-ons, integrate them well, and scale beyond the founder. The firms that achieve premium exits are rarely the ones with the best story at the time of sale. They are usually the ones who spent the previous 18 months building the evidence for it.

STRATEGY IS THE EASY PART. EXECUTION IS WHERE IT GETS REAL. The best strategies I've seen don't fail because the thinking was wrong. They failed because no one designed how the organisation should run them. Strategy and Process are treated as sequential in most organisations. Strategy first. Process later. That's the problem. By the time process catches up, the momentum is gone- and so is the alignment. The transformations that actually stick are the ones where both are designed together from Day one. Here's what process excellence does for strategy that most leaders underestimate: 1. It forces specificity: Vague strategies survive until someone has to operationalise them. Process design exposes every assumption the slide glossed over. 2. It creates accountability: A process without an owner is a wish. Build it into the design- not as an afterthought. 3. It makes change last: Consultants leave. Priorities shift. A well designed process outlasts both. That's the real deliverable. The most underrated consulting skill isn't strategic thinking. It's knowing how to make strategy stick.

Is consulting dying? I particularly don't think so. But the model that built the industry is. For decades, consulting ran on a simple formula. Deploy smart people. Bill their hours. Scale by hiring more smart people. Repeat. It worked. For a long time, it worked extremely well. But that formula is under more pressure right now than at any point in the industry's history. And I think it's worth being honest about that. Budget cuts are real. Clients who used to greenlight large transformation programs without much friction are now scrutinizing every line item. And then there's AI. But not in the way most people frame it. AI isn't replacing consultants. If anything, it's doing the opposite - it's stripping away the noise and forcing a more honest conversation about what consulting actually is. The hours spent on formatting, on pulling data, on building the first draft of a proposal that used to take a full day? That's being automated. And good riddance. Because what's left is the part that actually matters. The judgment call in a room where the data points in two directions. The ability to read an organisation and understand why the real problem is never the stated problem. The storytelling that turns a complex analysis into a decision a leadership team can align around. The trust built over years that makes a client pick up the phone before they even issue an RFP. That can't be automated. And frankly, it never should have been buried under billable hours in the first place. So, going back to the topic... the pressure the industry is feeling right now isn't a threat to consulting. It's a correction.

The GTM strategy I was most confident in — completely failed. Here's what it cost, what I rebuilt, and what I'd never do again. 2 years into a major EMEA market expansion. The ICP was defined. The positioning was sharp. The pipeline was moving. Then — nothing converted. Quarter after quarter. Same pipeline. Zero velocity. The board was asking questions I couldn't fully answer. The CEO was patient. For a while. Here's what I got wrong: 1. I confused market familiarity with market readiness. I knew the geography. I didn't know the buying committee had changed. 2. I built the GTM motion for the product we had — not the product the market needed. A 3-month listening gap would have saved 18 months of execution. 3. I moved to anchor partnerships before the narrative was battle-tested. Barclays-level conversations require Barclays-level proof points. We didn't have them yet. What we rebuilt: 90-day re-diagnosis with no assumptions carried forward. New ICP built entirely from closed-won interviews, not internal hypotheses. Narrative stress-tested against 12 cold C-suite conversations before any warm door was opened. The outcome: $15M in contracts closed in the 12 months after the rebuild. The lesson: the GTM that fails fastest is the one you were most certain about.

I spoke to 10 consultants last week. All of them were doing the work. None of them had a business yet. They were posting. Networking. Downloading LinkedIn playbooks. Trying to fix visibility. But when I asked them three questions: What's the root cause of the problem you solve? Who will actually pay to get it solved? What changes for them after working with you? Silence. Not because they weren't capable. Because they'd never defined those things. So they were trying to market something that didn't exist yet. That's why the content feels forced. The sales conversations feel awkward. The pricing feels like a guess. You're not marketing too little. You're marketing something that isn't defined yet. Your expertise is not your business. It's raw material. What makes it valuable is getting clear on the expensive problem you're known for solving, the person who will actually pay to get it solved, and the outcome that happens because of your work. The people winning right now got clear on those three things first. Everything else - the content, the conversations, the clients - compounded from there. Most consultants don't have a marketing problem. They have a definition problem. I'm building something for the people who are ready to stop theorizing and get in the market. Eight weeks. Real conversations. Real inputs. A business you've actually tested. More on that soon.

A client had 6 testimonials on their site. Conversion rate: 2.1%. Great testimonials too. Specific results. Named clients. Real transformations. Buried at the bottom of the page where nobody scrolled. We audited their site to find the pattern. Visitors landed, scanned the headline, read the first few lines of copy, hit a moment of doubt, and bounced. The testimonials that could have answered that doubt were four scrolls away. They had social proof. It was just in the wrong place at the wrong time. The psychology nobody talks about: Testimonials don't create trust. They answer doubt. And doubt doesn't wait until the bottom of your page. It shows up in the first 30 seconds. 'Is this legit?' 'Will this work for someone like me?' 'What if I'm not the right fit?' These questions flash through your prospect's mind while they're still above the fold. If the answer isn't there, they leave before scrolling. By the time they reach your testimonials, they've already decided. The social proof arrives after the verdict. Where this breaks down: Testimonials grouped in a section labeled 'What Our Clients Say.' Social proof placed after the offer instead of before the objection. Generic praise that doesn't address specific doubts. All testimonials saying the same thing instead of answering different concerns. Each one feels organized. Each one misses the moment.

Julius had been posting for 6+ months just to get crickets. I told him to 'niche down'. Here's what happened next: Leads started coming in within a week. Not from new content. From the same content — pointed at the right person. So before you stress about hooks, posting times, or what the algorithm wants this month — figure out who you help, what you help them with, and what makes you different. If you've been posting without seeing results, stop. Fix the foundation first before you burn yourself out with even more content. I built a simple protocol to 'reset' your LinkedIn and get leads within 7 days. It's free, just reply PROTOCOL below and I'll send it to you.

98% of megaprojects suffer cost overruns of more than 30%. 77% are at least 40% late. And still, the most dangerous moment in a project is not when the dashboard turns red. It is when the dashboard stays green because the truth has already been edited. I have seen this pattern many times. At first, nothing looks dramatic. The report is issued on time. The steering committee happens. The milestone is shown as recoverable. The forecast still looks acceptable. The language remains calm. But inside the project, people already know. A vendor data delay is becoming critical. A late approval is quietly consuming float. A change is being discussed commercially, but not reflected in the schedule. A productivity issue is hidden behind extra manpower. A risk is being monitored instead of being escalated. Everyone knows the numbers are drifting. No one has said it out loud yet. That silence is the most expensive moment in the project. Not because the problem is unfixable. Usually it still is. But because every day of silence is a day the cost to recover goes up. The projects that end badly are rarely the ones where something went technically wrong. They are the ones where the early signals were visible and the conversations did not happen.

I've watched the same $10M+ in unrecovered costs show up on project after project. It almost always starts in the first 90 days. I've seen this play out more times than I can count. A team wins a major project. Mobilisation starts. Everyone is focused on getting delivery moving. The commercial setup gets treated as something to sort out once things settle down. Things never settle down. By the time the first dispute surfaces, 6 months in, the damage was done in the first 90 days. Before the first notice was issued. Before the first variation was raised. Sometimes before the first meeting with the client. 4 things that go wrong in the first 90 days: 1. Scope assumptions carry over from the bid — the bid team made assumptions to win the work, and the delivery team inherits them without knowing which ones are solid. Nobody has that conversation. 2. The baseline is never locked — schedule and cost baselines get treated as drafts, and you lose the ability to measure variance. 3. Commercial setup gets deprioritised — contract admin, notice obligations, and change management are treated as bureaucracy instead of commercial protection. 4. Client relationships start wrong — the wrong tone in the first 90 days creates a defensive dynamic that's hard to unwind. The fix isn't complex. It's discipline in the first 90 days, before anyone believes there's a problem.

Early in my consulting career, a client told me something I didn't expect: He was pleasantly surprised with the final product because at the start of the project, I was difficult to work with. I was genuinely caught off guard. So I asked what he meant. It came down to this: He wanted an outcome his data couldn't support. Then he wanted something else I knew would create zero business value — but looked impressive. I had a few options. I could deliver something flashy that no one would use. I could stretch the project out, keep iterating, and keep billing. A lot of consultants do exactly that. Instead, I pushed back. Harder than was comfortable at the time. Not because I wanted to be difficult. Because I had no interest in delivering something that wouldn't matter. Eventually, we aligned. We built something grounded in the data, tied to real decisions, and designed to be used. Years later, I'm told it's still part of their daily workflow. That experience shaped how I work. I don't just produce outputs. I push for the right problem to be solved, even when that's inconvenient. Especially when that's inconvenient. That is the work. The rest is just execution.

If you run an IT consulting/services firm, you have probably lived through some version of this. Around 2023, there was a period at iQuasar where almost every leadership meeting ended the same way. Someone would say, 'Wait, which sheet is the correct one?' And for a few seconds, the room would go quiet. Not because people were careless. Quite the opposite. Everybody was working hard. Probably too hard. Sales had one hiring assumption. Delivery had another understanding of bandwidth. Recruitment was trying to fill roles that kept changing priority every few days. Finance had a different utilization view entirely. At one point, one of our delivery managers admitted he had started maintaining his own parallel tracker because he no longer fully trusted the central one. I wish I could say that was unusual. It wasn't. For years, we thought we were being scrappy and efficient. In hindsight, I think we were slowly building operational debt. The fix wasn't a new tool. It wasn't a process overhaul. It was getting everyone looking at the same numbers, with the same definitions, at the same time. That one change — shared operational truth — made every conversation faster, every decision cleaner, and every forecast more honest.

Truth I had to learn the hard way: Most consultants think narrowing their focus will shrink their pipeline. It won't — it will do the opposite, fast. The consultant who says 'I help mid-size companies with operational efficiency' gets polite nods. The consultant who says 'I help SaaS companies cut their customer onboarding time in half before they hit 100 employees' gets pulled into a conversation. Same expertise. Different specificity. Here's what's actually happening: Vague positioning forces the buyer to do work. They have to figure out if you're relevant. Most won't bother. Specific positioning does the work for them. The right person immediately thinks 'that's me.' The wrong person self-selects out. That second part feels like loss. It isn't. Every conversation you have with the wrong client is a conversation you're not having with the right one. Specificity doesn't shrink your market. It focuses your energy on the part of the market that will actually buy. If you're struggling to get traction, the problem is almost never that you need more leads. It's that you need fewer of the wrong ones. Narrow down. Watch what happens.

There's a move that builds more trust than any sales conversation I know of, and almost no consultant will make it. Tell the prospect they're not ready to work with you yet. Not as a close. Not as a trick. Genuinely. Map where they are, describe what they're going to start experiencing in the next few months, and tell them when it'll actually make sense to call. Then let them go. Most consultants can't do this because it feels like leaving money on the table. But watch what happens six months later, when everything you described is playing out exactly the way you said it would. They don't go looking for a consultant. This edition of Consulting Mastery is worth your time — it's the foundation of how I teach consultants to build a pipeline that converts without chasing.

A PSA For Working With Government. I've had a bit of a unique career path. I've worked in industry. I've worked as a consultant. I spent several years working in government. And now, I'm back in consulting again. Having seen all sides of the table, I wanted to share a quick and genuinely positive reminder for anyone who works with government or regulators. Most government workers truly want to help. During my time as a public servant, I worked alongside incredibly dedicated, thoughtful professionals. People who cared deeply about doing their jobs well, applying policy fairly, and helping people navigate complex processes. Government work isn't always easy. Public servants are often balancing legislation, policy, public interest, tight timelines, and high expectations — all at once. And they do it while interacting with people who are under stress, facing uncertainty, or dealing with high-stakes projects. What made the biggest difference in every interaction I've seen go well — whether as a consultant, an industry professional, or a public servant — was this: treat every person across the table as a professional trying to do a genuinely difficult job, not as an obstacle. That shift in mindset changes the tone of every meeting, email, and project review. It doesn't cost anything. And it makes everything move faster.

Most consultants have valuable ideas... which often don't make it out of their head. They're too busy delivering, too uncomfortable writing, and often unsure where to begin. I've seen brilliant people stay invisible on LinkedIn for years because the 'getting started' on content creation felt like an insurmountable wall. So my team built a system to remove that wall entirely. The client drops their raw thoughts into a Google Sheet — a conversation they had, an observation from a client call, an idea that's been rattling around for weeks. Our process takes those raw thoughts and turns them into LinkedIn content aligned with their voice, their brand, and their audience. Very little writing required on their end. Once a week I hop on a 20-minute call with each client to extract the ideas that week. Everything else is handled. The clients who get the best results aren't the ones with the most to say — they're the ones who stopped waiting until they had time to say it. If you're a consultant with real expertise and an empty content calendar, that's the problem we solve. DM me 'IDEAS' and I'll show you how it works.

I pose as a CIO when I audit my clients' LinkedIn profiles. I actually go through their last 180-day posts the way a decision maker would before deciding whether to reply to their outreach. Here is what I am looking for: The first thing I check is whether this person has a point of view on my world, or just their own product. A founder posting about their feature releases and funding rounds is talking to themselves. A founder posting about why enterprise procurement cycles are broken, why AI implementations stall at the security review, why the buying committee is the real product problem — that founder is talking to me. That is the first filter. Most do not pass it. The second thing I check is consistency of thought. Do these posts connect to each other? Is there a thread? Or does each post feel like a different person wrote it? The third is whether they have made me curious about them specifically. Not their category. Not their topic. Them. This is rare. If all three are present, I reply to their DM. Often the same day. If you want to know whether your LinkedIn passes this audit, I do these reviews for a small number of clients. DM me and I'll tell you what a CIO actually sees when they land on your profile.

£140k in new revenue from LinkedIn. Here's what actually changed for Richard, a B2B growth consultant, 6 months in. Before: 920 connections. Posting occasionally. No real system. New business came from his network and referrals. After: 2,580 connections. 8% reply rate on outreach. 5 deals closed, averaging £28k each. Total investment: £1,290. Return: 108x. So I asked Richard what had changed. He said, 'the big change is that I'm no longer starting from zero every time a project ends. There's always something warming up in the background.' That's the thing most consultants don't have: continuity and consistency in their marketing. Your sales pipeline doesn't wait for you to have capacity — leads aren't hanging around; it needs to build while you're delivering. If you're running a consulting firm and still relying on referrals and your existing network to fill pipeline, Richard's situation was probably familiar. The difference between where he was and where he is now isn't talent. It's having a system that runs whether he's head-down on a project or not.

CFOs. CEOs. COOs. Companies don't lose customers the day the contract ends. They lose them months earlier. That's when: calls stop getting returned as fast, issues start getting tolerated instead of solved, meetings get shorter, and your customer quietly begins looking around. But internally? Revenue still looks fine. Dashboards still look stable. Forecasts still look manageable. So leadership assumes everything is under control. That assumption is where the damage starts. A company can lose 5% of its customers every month and still convince itself things are mostly okay. Except 5% monthly churn compounds into roughly 46% annual customer loss. At 8-9% monthly churn? You can wipe out 65-70% of your customer base in a year. That is not a customer service problem. That's a boardroom problem. And the dangerous part is this: Most customers never formally complain. ThinkJar research found that 96% of unhappy customers don't complain directly to the company. They just quietly leave. By the time your dashboards catch it, the exit decision was already made. The customers you think you're keeping may have already left in everything but name.

Executive presence on LinkedIn isn't for everyone. That's not something you'll hear from most people selling LinkedIn services. Here's the honest version. If you're not willing to put yourself into the content — your perspective, your experience, your actual point of view — you'll probably spend a lot of time sounding like everyone else and wondering why it isn't working. If sharing a real opinion makes you want to post a conference photo instead, this will be hard. And if you need it to pay off in 90 days, don't start. The timeline is longer than most people want to admit. What executive presence on LinkedIn actually is: a 12-18 month project of consistently showing how you think, building a body of work that compounds, and becoming the person decision-makers associate with your space. I've watched partners at professional services firms do this from zero and get invited to pitch work they never would have seen before. It works. It just works slowly, and only if the perspective is real. If you're a B2B consulting or professional services leader who is willing to play a longer game, that's exactly the work I do. DM me if you want to understand what the first 90 days actually look like.

Got a business? Welcome to a world of problems. Meaning, running a business is simply problem solving, over and over. But something I discuss with my clients is having better problems than the ones we first started talking about. See if you can relate to any of the problems towards the end of the list, and if so, be grateful about how far you've already come. 1. I don't have any clients, I don't know where my next paycheck is coming from. 2. I don't know how to price this opportunity I've been presented with. 3. I don't know how to manage my time effectively across my clients, marketing, and development. 4. I don't know how to find the language to describe the work I do. 5. I don't know who my ideal client is. 6. I keep attracting the wrong clients. 7. I know what to do but I can't seem to take consistent action. 8. I have clients but I'm working too many hours for not enough money. 9. I'm getting referrals but I want to attract clients who find me first. 10. I have a full practice but I want to grow without adding more hours. 11. I want to raise prices but I'm nervous about it. If you're somewhere in that list, you're not behind. You're in exactly the right place to take the next step. That's the work I do — helping consultants, coaches, and advisors move from wherever they are to the next problem worth having. DM me if you want to talk about where you're stuck.

One of our clients pays us multi 6-figures per year. We're able to charge that much for 3 reasons: 1. We serviced them for over 3 years. 2. They saw consistent growth over time. 3. We have a performance based agreement. That last part is very important. Most agencies only charge flat retainers because it's predictable cashflow for them. The brand pays the same whether the account scales or sits flat for 6 months. That model has one fatal flaw: the agency gets paid the same whether they're crushing it or coasting, and so most agencies coast. That's why we always include a performance based fee in our contractual agreements. This pricing model isn't for every agency. But if you want to scale to 7 figures without churning a large number of clients every month — it's your only option. The agencies that keep clients the longest aren't the cheapest ones. They're the ones whose incentives are aligned with the client's growth.

A client started charging 5x more than her clients were used to paying. The result? A 31,245 EUR launch. Most people will look at this and think: her clients were willing to pay way more than expected. Not exactly. They were willing to pay more once the value made sense. That's the real point. People don't resist higher prices only because the number is bigger. They resist when the price feels disconnected from what they understand. This is where many experts get stuck. They have the skill. They have the experience. They have the ability to help. But the offer still sounds too loose, too similar to what people have seen before. So when they raise the price, it feels random. Not premium. Just more expensive. The fix is not a higher price. It's a clearer offer — one that connects what you do to the result the buyer actually wants. When that connection is clear, 5x doesn't feel like a lot. It feels like the obvious choice. If you're sitting on expertise that isn't converting at the price it deserves, DM me to talk about your best next revenue step.

Every client we've worked with comes to us with the same underlying problem. Revenue wasn't growing and nobody could agree on why. They thought they have a sales problem, a marketing problem, or my favorite — a 'we just need more pipeline' problem. They're wrong. What they actually have is a gap. The space between what leadership believes about their go-to-market and what's actually happening inside it. I've seen it kill quotas, careers, products, and companies. First, the VP gets blamed. Then the market. Then the product. Then the team. I've watched founders blame things that made zero sense, because the real answer was uncomfortable. The real answer was almost always the same. So we built a methodology around closing that gap. A repeatable way to surface what the business believes, measure what's actually happening, and rebuild the connection between the two. That's what we do for CEOs and founders when the revenue engine is broken and another hire isn't the answer. If that sounds like your situation, I'd be interested in a conversation.

I made a $47,000 mistake in 2025. Not a campaign. Not a bad client. A hire. Brilliant resume. Impressive numbers. Confident in the interview. But 90 days in, the team was quietly falling apart. Deliverables were slipping. Energy shifted. Two of my best people started going quiet in meetings. I couldn't figure it out. Then I went back and read his interview notes. Every single answer started with 'I.' 'I built this.' 'I scaled that.' 'I was responsible for...' Not once did he say 'we.' Not once did he mention the team behind the results. Not once did he talk about what he left better than he found it. I missed it completely. That hire cost me 4 months of cleanup. A client relationship. And the morale of people I genuinely cared about. I've made that mistake three times now. Same red flag. Every time. Here's what I listen for now: The best people don't talk about credit. They talk about contribution. They don't lead with titles. They lead with problems they solved. They don't say 'I built a team.' They say 'we figured out how to scale this.' The language someone uses in an interview is not small talk. It's a preview of how they'll show up in your culture. Read it carefully.

Everyone told me retainers were the smart move. Predictable income. Stable clients. Safe. So I made a bet that most agency owners won't. Here's what nobody says out loud: retainers let you hide. Mediocre results can slide for months before anyone notices. You invoice, they pay, and the awkward conversation never happens. I lived it. I had a retainer client early on. Did the bare minimum. Sent the report. Collected the check. They weren't unhappy. But they weren't growing. Neither was I. The retainer wasn't just a pricing decision. It was a comfort zone. So I switched. Pay per booked appointment. Small setup fee. Minimal tool costs. Nothing else. If I don't deliver, I don't get paid. Every campaign has to be dialed in. No buffer. No hiding behind 'we're still optimizing.' Something shifted when I stopped having a safety net. I stopped coasting and started caring about every single lead. The result? Better outcomes. Longer client relationships. And a business model where my incentives are finally aligned with the client's growth. If you want to know how the model works, DM me.

If you're a coach or a consultant, you might feel like other coaches are your competitors. Actually, they might be your secret weapon. I know so many folks who pride themselves on not knowing or hanging out with other coaches: 'I only spend time with clients and prospects.' But that's a mistake. The truth is, most coaches or consultants don't lose out on business because the client chooses to work with someone else. They're not calling you back because of these 4 things. You haven't established enough value — they're just not sure it's worth the hassle of bringing someone in for an uncertain result. You're not memorable enough. You met once, had a good conversation, but then disappeared. When their need becomes urgent, they can't quite remember your name or what made you different. You lack social proof. They don't see evidence that people like them have worked with you and gotten great results. You're not top of mind. You've done nothing to stay in contact or ensure they remember you exist. Here's what most successful consultants know that others don't: peer relationships with other coaches aren't competition. They're referral pipelines, thinking partners, and the fastest way to build the credibility and community that keeps you top of mind. If you want to talk through how to build that network strategically, DM me.

'Everyone tells me my work is incredible. But nobody buys.' A nervous system regulation coach said that to me on our first call. And it is the most common sentence I hear from experts. Every. Single. Week. They have testimonials. They have referrals that rave about them. Their clients get transformational results. People comment on their posts saying 'this is exactly what I needed to hear.' And then nothing happens. No DM. No booking. No sale. After hearing this from hundreds of experts, I can tell you it always points to the same root cause. You are being appreciated. You are not being positioned as essential. There is a massive difference between content that makes people think 'she is brilliant' and content that makes people think 'I cannot keep going without this.' This coach's content was warm, insightful, genuinely valuable. Her audience loved her. But every piece of content answered a question her audience had — instead of making them feel the cost of not having her help. That is the shift. Stop creating content that makes you look good. Start creating content that makes your audience feel the gap. When people can feel what it costs them to stay where they are, they stop appreciating your work from a distance and start wanting to close that gap. That is what I help coaches and consultants build — a brand message that makes you essential, not just excellent. DM me 'ESSENTIAL' and I'll show you where your positioning is leaking.
